channel.title: Zeteo
channel.description: Mehdi Hasan's Zeteo — First Draft and news analysis

item.title: Reza Pahlavi's Movement Is Dangerous. I've Seen It Up Close
item.description: A note from our Editor-in-Chief: This isn’t a voice you normally see on Zeteo. Alireza Nader is a former fellow at the neoconservative, hawkishly pro-Israel Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD) and an Iranian who supports regime change in Iran. He was also once friendly with Reza…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
A note from our Editor-in-Chief:

This isn’t a voice you normally see on Zeteo. Alireza Nader is a former fellow at the neoconservative, hawkishly pro-Israel Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD) and an Iranian who supports regime change in Iran. He was also once friendly with Reza Pahlavi, but he now believes the son of the last shah of Iran is fronting a dangerous, anti-democratic movement. While Zeteo does not share all of the views expressed in this article, we believe this is a rarely-reported perspective on Pahlavi and his supporters and, therefore, this is an important piece.

-Mehdi

It was in 2017 that I first met Reza Pahlavi, the son of the late shah of Iran, at an Italian restaurant in Potomac, Maryland, near where he lived. Our meeting took place right after the mass protests that year began in Iran and after I had left my job at the RAND Corporation, freeing me to advocate for democracy in Iran. I had been particularly impressed with Pahlavi’s passionate defense of the protesters during an interview on Al Jazeera. I had never been a fan of the monarchy in Iran, but I wanted to meet him.

He was an affable and down-to-earth man who genuinely seemed to care about Iran. We spoke at length about democracy and freedom for all Iranians. I believed in Pahlavi’s talk of democracy because it was backed by action at the time. A broad coalition of liberal democrats and even progressives had gathered around him, in addition to the usual constitutional monarchists who had always been the core of his support base.

But Pahlavi’s movement morphed into something much darker and more dangerous over the years, especially after he effectively dropped out of a coalition formed by eight prominent Iranian opposition members at Georgetown University in 2023 and declared himself the sole leader of the Iranian opposition. A few months later, I had lost all faith in the former crown prince and his movement, especially after I observed his staffers plot against other opponents of the regime and treat even their allies with contempt and derision.

To understand how extremist and increasingly violent the Pahlavist movement has become, look no further than the recent murder of Canadian-Iranian democracy activist Masood Masjoody in British Columbia.

Masjoody, a former supporter of Pahlavi, had become a fierce critic of him and his movement. The two people who have been charged with Masjoody’s murder, authorities say, were known to him. Before his death, Masjoody claimed that the two suspects were Pahlavist fanatics who were plotting to kill him using a substance such as poison.

At the time of his death, Masjoody was involved in active litigation in a Vancouver court against Pahlavi and his supporters, including his accused murderers. While the motive for the killing remains a matter of intense public speculation and ongoing police investigation (there’s no evidence at this point that Pahlavi or his advisers were in any way involved), Masjoody’s public profile as a vocal critic of the Pahlavist movement has made the case a focal point for those concerned about the safety of Iranian dissidents, especially after death threats and even physical attacks against Pahlavi critics.

The Pahlavist Threat

Pahlavi attempts to portray himself as a democrat and as a “transitional” leader in a post-regime Iran, but the movement he “leads” has exhibitedalarming behavior.

Pahlavi and his inner circle had been accused by dissidents such as Masjoody of conducting a campaign of intimidation and threats against the rest of the Iranian opposition to silence dissent and position Pahlavi as the only alternative to the current regime.

The Pahlavists are closely supported by elements linked to the Israeli government and Persian-language broadcasts such as Iran International, which has promoted him heavily during the current war and even conducted a defection campaign for him in which potential regime defectors were told to scan a QR Code broadcast by Iran International in order to register their defection in a Google document. Pahlavi later stated that up to 100,000 regime supporters had defected, although no evidence of this claim has emerged.

The cooperation between Pahlavi and the Israeli government appears to have grown stronger since Pahlavi left the Georgetown coalition, and as his movement has taken on the characteristics of illiberal and far-right movements in Israel and across the world.

The Pahlavist movement’s shift toward more rigid, exclusionary rhetoric has been closely associated with advisers such as Amir Etemadi and Saeed Ghasseminejad, the latter of whom is a senior adviser at the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD). While they are presented as advisers, their behavior is often more akin to handlers who direct the movement’s strategic and ideological path.

Both Etemadi and Ghasseminejad have consistently attacked other members of the opposition, claiming that whoever opposes Pahlavi is an “ethnic separatist” or a “leftist.” Etemadi has even called Iranian journalists at Iran International “terrorists” (before the broadcast became heavily pro-Pahlavi) and attacked dissident Ruhollah Zam, who was eventually lured to Iran and executed by the regime.

Ghasseminejad has stated that “you’re either with Prince Reza Pahlavi or the Islamic Republic,”has written about “the tree branches and lamp posts on Pahlavi Avenue (in Tehran) that have been barren for years,” and has advocated that Israel adopt the death penalty for Palestinians, possibly mimicking such figures as the Israeli far-right Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir.

Much of the harassment and threats are conducted over social media, including on X, but as Masjoody’s case demonstrates, the threats against Pahlavi’s critics are very real.

Masjoody had belonged to an opposition group led by Etemadi called Iran Revival, and was alarmed by the group’s activities.

Masjoody also claimedthat his former associates at Iran Revival were connected to the Revolutionary Guards, a widespread rumor within the Iranian diaspora. Pahlavi’s claims that he had significant support within the regime have only fueled the rumors.

What is more troubling than rumor is the political model reflected in Pahlavi’s published transition plan, which critics, including me, view as overly centralized and weak on independent oversight. The plan, drafted with Ghasseminejad as a lead author and implementer, provides Pahlavi with substantial power rather than building clear checks and balances through credible institutions.

An earlier version also drew criticism for proposing a council whose membership was not publicly disclosed, raising concerns about transparency and accountability. Although that provision was later removed after public backlash, the broader concern remains that the framework did not convincingly answer how power would be restrained during a transition.

Moreover, Ghasseminejad’s employment at FDD and his role in implementing the transition plan are clearly a conflict of interest, as FDD’s mission is not to protect the interests of the Iranian people.

A Useful Israeli Instrument

The war between Israel and Iran demonstrates the utility of Pahlavi for the current Israeli government, which seeks to achieve Israel’s primary national interests, the destruction of Iran’s nuclear and military capabilities, rather than the overthrow of the regime. The Netanyahu government is smart enough to know that Pahlavi is unlikely to become the shah or even the leader of the opposition. Rather, Pahlavi is a useful Israeli instrument in its war against the regime.

For example, Pahlavi called on his supporters to go out into the streets and take over regime institutions in January 2026 after peaceful protests and strikes had broken out in Iran’s bazaars independently of him. The Netanyahu government, more interested in destroying Iran’s military and nuclear capabilities, is likely to have viewed peaceful protests and strikes as an ineffectual strategy. The Israeli government also reportedly knew that any attempt to take over government buildings by unarmed Iranians would lead to a massacre by the regime, though Pahlavi and Iran International urged Iranians to do so anyhow.

The regime reacted to Pahlavi’s calls for a mass uprising by massacring thousands of unarmed Iranian protesters on Jan. 8 and 9, even as Pahlavi and Iran International called for more people to go into the streets on Jan. 11!

Pahlavi has again called for his “Immortal Guard” to take to the streets as the US and Israel bomb military and civilian targets across the country. Hopefully, fewer Iranians will listen to him this time.

A Movement that Cannot Be Rescued

So, what is the solution to the Pahlavist dilemma? The movement has irreparably morphed into a rigid and dogmatic cult of personality. It cannot be rescued. Pahlavi is a hostage of his own inner circle and has become a tool of the Netanyahu government. But he also deserves full blame for his own actions, which have divided the Iranian opposition and induced an atmosphere of fear among the diaspora, even in Washington, DC.

Pahlavi has been repeatedly warned to cease his political activities by friends and associates, but he is likely to continue on his disastrous path as long as he remains the center of attention and applause.

Masjoody was one of the few people in the diaspora who was openly critical of Pahlavi and wanted Iranians to know the truth about his fanatical cult.

I was in contact with him before his murder; he even used my research on monarchists in his court testimony against Pahlavi.

Pahlavi continues to present himself to global audiences as Iran’s savior. Masjoody knew better and tried very hard to make everyone see the truth. When will the world see what he saw?

Alireza Nader is an independent scholar of Iran and the Middle East based in Washington, DC.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Corporate Media “Happily Manufacturing Consent” for Iran War- So What Are You Going to Do About It?
item.description: We saw it with Gaza and we’re seeing it again in Iran. Legacy media is failing us when it comes to holding power to account for the devastating US-Israeli war in Iran. From softball CBS interviews, to the New York Times burying the attack on a girls school on page 11, Mehdi says leading media…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
We saw it with Gaza and we’re seeing it again in Iran. Legacy media is failing us when it comes to holding power to account for the devastating US-Israeli war in Iran. From softball CBS interviews, to the New York Times burying the attack on a girls school on page 11, Mehdi says leading media outlets are “happily manufacturing consent for this devastating and illegal war.”

But at Zeteo, we’re leading with the “blunt honesty and moral clarity” you’re searching for. Listen to Mehdi play out exactly how Zeteo is pushing back against corporate media and why more than ever your subscriber support matters.

item.title: The MAGA Elite Is Being Torn Apart by Trump’s War in Iran
item.description: A counterterrorism official with ties to domestic extremists resigned from Donald Trump’s administration on Tuesday, marking the first major departure over the US-Israeli war in Iran that has exposed a fissure in the broader MAGA coalition and its elite influencers and policy hands. Joe Kent,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded: A counterterrorism official with ties to domestic extremists resigned from Donald Trump’s administration on Tuesday, marking the first major departure over the US-Israeli war in Iran that has exposed a fissure in the broader MAGA coalition and its elite influencers and policy hands. Joe Kent, director of the National Counterterrorism Center, announced his exit on Twitter after just seven months on the job. “I cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war in Iran,” he wrote. “Iran posed no imminent threat to our nation, and it is clear that we started this war due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby.” Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene, an ex-Trump confidant and one of the most outspoken conservative critics of Trump’s illegal war, immediately rallied behind Kent, along with commentators Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens. “They are going to lie about Joe Kent and try to discredit him. Do not believe the lies!” Greene tweeted. “Joe Kent is a great American hero deployed 11 times and a Gold Star Husband!” His service aside, we must note that Kent never should have been in this position in the first place, given his extensive ties to far-right extremists, his own noxious policy positions, and his support for Trump’s anti-democratic lies about the 2020 election – though none of these are considered bad things in the Trump administration. And Kent was, it seems, fine with being part of Team Trump during this president’s other wars of aggression, both at home and abroad. Now that he’s left the cult, Trumpworld is laying into Kent. Former senior Trump White House aide Taylor Budowich called Kent a “crazed egomaniac,” adding: “This isn’t some principled resignation – he just wanted to make a splash before getting canned. What a loser.” Trump attacked Kent on his way out as well. “I always thought he was weak on security,” Trump said Tuesday of the man he appointed as his top counterterrorism official. “When I read the statement, I realized that it’s a good thing that he’s out.” Opposing ‘Endless Wars’ The Trump White House would like the American public to believe that this Kent fellow is just a disgruntled, useless, appointee – and a persistent leaker who should have been fired – and not representative of a broader discontent among MAGAville’s upper echelons. They also want you to believe that everyone who matters in the administration is fully supportive of this president’s war and that there’s no daylight among the Cabinet level. This simply isn’t true. One reason it’s not true is because for it to be true, Cabinet members would have had to be for the war before Trump started it. According to various sources with direct knowledge of the matter and others briefed on the situation, shockingly few at the highest levels – save for President Trump – were gung-ho about launching the war in its run-up. Instead, many prominent members of Trump’s family, outside-adviser orbit, and the Trump-Vance administration (including Vice President JD Vance) defaulted to a go-along-to-get-along position. In internal deliberations, including ones with the president in the room, they sat on the fence. They didn’t push too hard in any particular direction. They just wanted to signal that they would do what Trump wanted, and that once he made the call, they’d work overtime to sell it. And with the war having entered its third, horrifying week, several top Trump officials and Cabinet members, several of the sources say, have only grown more pessimistic about the US’s chances in achieving any real or worthwhile “victory” in the conflict. Some high-ranking personnel whisper to one another about how they are utterly convinced that the war will damage the GOP’s already tenuous-at-best chances in the 2026 midterms – election outcomes that Trump himself stresses are absolutely crucial to the fate of his political agenda. Vanishingly few, however, are talking about quitting in protest. “I’m not going anywhere,” one senior administration official tells Zeteo, adding that the war was “a bad idea” and “a lot of people” in the government don’t think it’s going well and are unhappy that the war is being prioritized right now over other MAGA domestic policy initiatives. (Read: Trump’s ethnic-cleansing campaign.) Extremist Ties As various conservative factions weigh in on Kent’s opposition to the Iran war, they are all glossing over one key detail – that his history of cozying up to domestic extremists and amplifying conspiracy theories should have disqualified him from being appointed to his post to begin with. At his confirmation hearing, Kent – a 2020 election denier – repeated a ridiculous conspiracy theory that federal agents helped plan and direct the Jan. 6 riot at the US Capitol. He has also faced heat for his ties to alt-right white nationalists, including Holocaust denier Nick Fuentes; Joey Gibson, founder of the Christian nationalist group Patriot Prayer; and Proud Boy Graham Jorgenson, whom he paid $11,000 for consulting on Kent’s 2022 failed congressional campaign. Like many top Trump officials, Kent previously claimed that Trump would “end the wars” upon being elected president again in 2024. When Trump picked Tulsi Gabbard as his director of national intelligence, Kent applauded the move, writing that “Tulsi understands how to defend our nation by not getting us involved in endless wars.” As director of the National Counterterrorism Center, Kent served as a top aide to Gabbard. Unlike Kent, Gabbard has not handed in her resignation and remains a top official within the administration. She has had virtually nothing to say about Trump’s war, even though he is waging the exact type of war she’s always spoken out against. On Wednesday, Gabbard registered extremely measured support for Trump’s war in Iran, with a tweet that read like it was written at gunpoint. “Donald Trump was overwhelmingly elected by the American people to be our President and Commander in Chief,” she wrote. “As our Commander in Chief, he is responsible for determining what is and is not an imminent threat, and whether or not to take action he deems necessary to protect the safety and security of our troops, the American people and our country.” She added, “After carefully reviewing all the information before him, President Trump concluded that the terrorist Islamist regime in Iran posed an imminent threat and he took action based on that conclusion. Gabbard for years railed against US-led wars in the Middle East – and she was adamantly opposed to the notion of a war with Iran. During her 2020 campaign, she even sold T-shirts proclaiming in big, bold letters: “No War With Iran.” During the 2024 campaign, she billed Trump as “a man who wants to end wars, not start them.” Base on Board Kent’s departure signals the deepening rift within the MAGA elite over Trump’s war of choice in the Middle East. Axios recently reported that “some in [Trump’s] inner circle have what one official called ‘buyer’s remorse’ – growing fears that attacking Iran was a mistake.” This is partially true in the sense that they very much went along with it – but few, if any, bought into the idea that it was a good idea, as Trump, Lindsey Graham, Sean Hannity, and Israel’s Benjamin Netanyahu did. Today, that dynamic persists. There are next to zero profiles in courage to be found. The most senior levels of the federal government are still dutifully carrying out what the Mad King commands, even if they know it’s the orders of an increasingly Mad King. And yet, the discontent is real. If this war stretches on much longer, growing intra-MAGA displeasure and frustrations will only continue to bubble up to the surface, no matter how much the Trump White House spins the public on how All Is Well. One Trump adviser tells Zeteo they are slightly surprised there haven’t been more resignations at this point, beyond Kent’s, given the internal consternations, and that they expect there to be more if this war goes “sideways.” But this isn’t a hypothetical scenario. In many ways, Trump’s war is already going sideways – and signs point to things getting worse. Trump’s illegal war in Iran might be causing discontent among the MAGA elite and prominent right-wing influencers, like Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens. And it might be causing Trump to bleed political support with the independents who helped him win in 2024. Trump’s base, however, is another story. While a majority of Americans oppose Trump’s handling of the war, 84% of Republican voters support it, according to a Quinnipiac University poll released March 9. A recent average of polls by Nate Silver’s team found that 40% of Americans approve of Trump’s war in Iran – but 77% of Republicans approve of his conflict. Still, there are some in the Trump administration who are predicting that this war could last even into the final months of the year. And there are, sources stress to Zeteo, senior Trump officials who practically take it as a given that this Republican approval number will go down, if Trump’s war begins to turn into a protracted quagmire. Check out more from Zeteo:
